| `publisher` | 1 | The entity responsible for making the resource available; always “Open Negative Results Registry” for ONRR publications. | Fixed value |
| `publicationYear` | 1 | The year of publication, for citation. | Derived |
| `resourceType` | 1 | The type of resource: `NegativeResult`, `PositiveResult`, `Replication`, `Dataset`, `Protocol`, `Collection`. | Controlled vocabulary |
| `subjects` | 0..N | Ontology terms representing the topic(s) of the research (e.g., diseases, phenomena). Each entry is an `OntologyTerm` value object. | Yes, ontologies |
| `keywords` | 0..N | Free‑text tags provided by the authors, complementing formal subjects. | No |
| `language` | 1 | The primary language of the resource content, using ISO 639‑3. | ISO 639‑3 |
| `relatedIdentifiers` | 0..N | Links to related resources (DOIs, ARKs, URLs) with a `relationType` (e.g., `IsSupplementTo`, `IsReplicationOf`, `Cites`). | Yes, relation type vocabulary |
| `version` | 0..1 | The version string of the resource, if applicable (for Datasets). | Semantic versioning |

Business Rules for Descriptive Metadata:

* `title` and `description` are mandatory for any resource that is submitted (MR‑01).  
* Every `creator` must have a verified ORCID (SR‑03) unless an exception is granted.  
* The `publisher` field is immutable after publication and is set to the ONRR registry.  
* `relatedIdentifiers` must be validated for resolvability (VAL‑MET‑05).

### **17.3.2 Administrative Metadata**

Administrative metadata supports the management, provenance, and governance of the resource.

| Field Name | Cardinality | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `rights` | 1 | The license under which the resource is made available, expressed as an SPDX identifier or Creative Commons URI. This is derived from the `License` value object. |
| `accessRights` | 1 | `Open`, `Embargoed`, or `Restricted`. Governs public visibility. |
| `embargoEndDate` | 0..1 | The date when an embargo lifts; only if `accessRights` \= `Embargoed`. |
| `fundingReferences` | 0..N | List of `Funding` value objects, each with `funderName`, `funderIdentifier` (FundRef ID), `awardNumber`, `awardTitle`. |
| `provenance` | 1..N | A set of `ProvenanceEntry` value objects detailing the origin and transformations of the resource, following the W3C PROV model. |
| `auditTrail` | 1 | Reference to the `AuditRecord` log that documents all state changes of the resource. |
| `governancePolicyVersion` | 1 | The version of the ONRR governance policy under which this resource was submitted and published. |
| `conflictOfInterestStatement` | 1 | Aggregated COI declarations of all authors. |
| `ethicsApproval` | 0..1 | If the underlying experiment required ethics approval, the `EthicsApproval` details are included. |
| `dataAccessCommittee` | 0..1 | If the dataset is restricted, reference to the DAC that governs access. |

Business Rules:

* `rights` must be a valid open license (preferred) or a restricted license with justification.  
* `embargoEndDate` must be consistent with the `Embargo` value object and not exceed maximum policy duration (SR‑09).  
* `fundingReferences` must be validated against FundRef (VAL‑MET‑05).  
* `governancePolicyVersion` is set at submission time and never changed, ensuring that the rules under which the resource was evaluated are preserved.

### **17.3.3 Structural Metadata**

Structural metadata describes the internal organisation of the resource, particularly for datasets.

| Field Name | Cardinality | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `fileFormats` | 0..N | List of MIME types or format identifiers (e.g., `CSV`, `FASTQ`, `DICOM`). |
| `size` | 0..1 | Total size in bytes of all files in the resource. |
| `checksum` | 0..1 | A `Checksum` value object for integrity verification. |
| `numberOfFiles` | 0..1 | If a dataset consists of multiple files, the count. |
| `observationCount` | 0..1 | The number of data points (for datasets). |

---

## **17.4 Discipline‑Specific Metadata Profiles**

The core schema captures universal information, but it cannot, and should not, attempt to encode the vast diversity of experimental details across disciplines. A clinical trial requires fields for phase, enrolment, and primary outcome; a particle physics experiment needs collision energy and luminosity; a social science survey needs sampling method and questionnaire instruments. To accommodate this diversity while keeping the core stable, ONRR adopts a Core‑plus‑Profiles architecture.

### **17.4.1 The MetadataProfile Entity**

A `MetadataProfile` is a domain entity (Chapter 8) that defines an additional set of metadata fields, validation rules, and ontology bindings for a specific scientific domain. Profiles are developed and maintained by domain expert communities under the oversight of the ONRR governance body.

| Attribute | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| `profileId` | Unique identifier (e.g., `biomedical-v2`, `socialsciences-v1`). |
| `name` | Human‑readable label. |
| `description` | Scope and intended use. |
| `version` | Semantic version of the profile; fields may be added or deprecated over time. |
| `fields` | A list of `ProfileField` definitions, each specifying the field name, cardinality, data type, controlled vocabulary/ontology binding, and validation constraints. |
| `parentProfileId` | Optional; profiles can extend other profiles (e.g., a `neuroscience-imaging` profile extends `biomedical`). |
| `governance` | The community group responsible for maintaining the profile. |
| `publicationStatus` | `Draft`, `Active`, `Deprecated`. |

When a researcher creates a submission, they select (or are assigned) the most appropriate active profile. The submission’s metadata record then conforms to both the core schema and the selected profile. The curation process verifies compliance.

### **17.4.2 Biomedical Profile (Exemplar)**

The Biomedical profile is the first and most developed profile, given ONRR’s initial focus on biomedical negative results. It adds fields that are critical for clinical and preclinical research.

| Field Name | Cardinality | Description | Ontology/Vocabulary |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `diseaseCondition` | 0..N | The disease(s) or condition(s) studied. | MONDO, DOID |
| `interventionType` | 0..1 | The type of intervention: `Drug`, `Device`, `Procedure`, `Behavioural`, `Dietary`, `Other`. | Controlled vocabulary |
| `interventionName` | 0..1 | The name of the specific drug, device, etc. | ChEBI, DrugBank, GMDN |
| `studyDesign` | 1 | The design of the study: `RCT`, `Cohort`, `CaseControl`, `CrossSectional`, `CaseReport`, `SystematicReview`, `MetaAnalysis`, `InVitro`, `AnimalModel`, `Other`. | OBI, EFO |
| `phase` | 0..1 | For clinical trials: `I`, `II`, `III`, `IV`, `Pilot`, `N/A`. | Controlled vocabulary |
| `trialRegistrationId` | 0..1 | The public trial registry identifier (NCT number, EudraCT number). | External registry |
| `enrollment` | 0..1 | Actual or target number of participants. |  |
| `primaryOutcome` | 0..N | The primary endpoint(s) of the study, each described with a name, measurement method, and time point. | OBI, EFO |
| `secondaryOutcome` | 0..N | Secondary endpoints. |  |
| `eligibilityCriteria` | 0..1 | Free‑text inclusion and exclusion criteria. |  |
| `sex` | 0..1 | The sex of the study population: `Male`, `Female`, `Mixed`, `NotApplicable`. |  |
| `ageRange` | 0..1 | Minimum and maximum age of participants. |  |
| `organism` | 0..N | For non‑human studies, the organism(s) used (e.g., Mus musculus). | NCBI Taxonomy |
| `strain` | 0..1 | The specific strain or cell line. |  |
| `modelType` | 0..1 | For animal models: `Knockout`, `Transgenic`, `Xenograft`, etc. | Controlled vocabulary |
| `biospecimenType` | 0..N | Type of biospecimen collected (e.g., blood, tissue). | OBI |
| `ethicsApprovalNumber` | 0..1 | The approval number from the ethics committee. |  |
| `safetyData` | 0..1 | Did the study collect safety data? (boolean) |  |
| `seriousAdverseEvents` | 0..1 | Summary of serious adverse events, if any. |  |

Validation Rules for Biomedical Profile:

* If `studyDesign` is `RCT` or any clinical design, `trialRegistrationId` is mandatory (VAL‑GOV‑05).  
* If `interventionType` is `Drug`, `interventionName` must be resolvable in ChEBI or DrugBank.  
* `diseaseCondition` terms must be from MONDO or DOID (VAL‑ONT‑04).

### **17.4.3 Social Sciences Profile (Conceptual)**

The social sciences profile is a planned extension that captures survey methodology, population sampling, and qualitative outcomes.

| Field Name | Cardinality | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `populationDescription` | 1 | The target population and sampling frame. |
| `samplingMethod` | 1 | `Random`, `Stratified`, `Convenience`, `Snowball`, etc. |
| `instrument` | 0..N | The questionnaire, survey, or measurement tool used, preferably with a persistent identifier. |
| `unitOfObservation` | 1 | `Individual`, `Household`, `Organization`, `Event`, etc. |
| `geographicCoverage` | 0..1 | The geographic area covered by the study (e.g., country, region). |
| `timePeriodCovered` | 0..1 | The temporal scope of the data collection. |
| `dataCollectionMethod` | 0..1 | `OnlineSurvey`, `TelephoneInterview`, `FaceToFace`, `AdministrativeData`, etc. |
| `responseRate` | 0..1 | The survey response rate. |
| `qualitativeOutcomes` | 0..N | For qualitative studies, the themes or phenomena observed. |

### **17.4.4 Physical Sciences Profile (Conceptual)**

This profile captures experimental parameters common in physics, chemistry, and materials science.

| Field Name | Cardinality | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `materialStudied` | 0..N | The chemical substance, compound, or material under investigation (e.g., via PubChem, InChI). |
| `propertyMeasured` | 0..N | The physical property measured (e.g., conductivity, melting point). |
| `experimentalTechnique` | 0..N | The technique or instrument used (e.g., X‑ray diffraction, NMR spectroscopy). |
| `conditions` | 0..N | Key experimental conditions (temperature, pressure, magnetic field) with units. |
| `computationalModel` | 0..1 | For simulations: the software, version, and model parameters. |
| `dataProcessingSteps` | 0..N | Description of data reduction, calibration, or analysis pipelines. |

---

## **17.5 Metadata Lifecycle and Versioning**

Metadata is not static; it evolves alongside the resource it describes. The ONRR domain explicitly models metadata changes through versioning and eventing.

### **17.5.1 Metadata State and Resource Lifecycle**

The `MetadataRecord` undergoes state transitions synchronised with the parent entity:

* Draft: When a `Submission` is in `Draft`, its metadata is mutable. Authors can freely add, remove, or modify fields. Validation warnings are displayed but do not block saving.  
* Submitted: When the `Submission` transitions to `Submitted`, the metadata record is validated against the core schema and profile. Any errors block submission. The metadata becomes immutable to authors; only curators may edit it.  
* UnderReview / RevisionsRequired: Curators may enrich metadata (e.g., adding ontology terms, correcting formats). Each curation action is logged and may increment a `metadataRevision` counter.  
* Accepted: Metadata is locked except for final polishing by the curator. The license, embargo, and publisher fields are frozen.  
* Published: The metadata record is frozen and a snapshot is attached to the `Publication`. Any subsequent correction to metadata (e.g., a typo in the title) must follow the publication versioning mechanism (a new `Publication` version with an updated metadata record, linked to the original).

### **17.5.2 Metadata Versioning**

For entities that support versioning natively (like `Dataset`), each version has its own `MetadataRecord`. The record includes a `version` field that matches the entity version. The `MetadataRecord` itself does not need independent versioning beyond what the parent entity provides. However, the `MetadataRecord` stores the `schemaVersion` and `profileId`/`profileVersion` that it was validated against, ensuring that the semantics of the metadata at the time of publication are preserved even if the schema evolves later (backward compatibility).

---

## **17.6 Metadata Validation and Quality**

Metadata quality is enforced through the validation rules detailed in Chapter 15. The `Metadata & Ontology` bounded context provides a validation service that can be invoked by the `Submission`, `Review & Curation`, and `Publication` contexts. The validation process generates a `ValidationResult` aggregate, which is stored and made available to the submitter and curators.

### **17.6.1 Automated Checks**

* Structural: Required fields present, cardinality constraints satisfied.  
* Syntactic: ORCID checksum, DOI format, ISO 8601 date validity.  
* Semantic: Ontology term resolvability and active status, controlled vocabulary membership.  
* Consistency: Embargo dates consistent with access rights, license compatibility with open‑access policy, grant number format matching funder.

### **17.6.2 Curator Manual Review**

Automated checks cannot assess the accuracy of a free‑text description or the appropriateness of a subject ontology term. Curators are responsible for manual review of semantic accuracy as part of the curation process. The `CurationActionTaken` events record any metadata corrections.

### **17.6.3 Metadata Completeness Score**

As a quality incentive, the system computes a `MetadataCompletenessScore` (0‑100) based on the presence of both mandatory and recommended fields. This score is displayed to authors during drafting and is available in the published record as part of the `DataQualityReport`. While not a blocking factor, it encourages thorough metadata entry.

---

## **17.7 Ontology and Controlled Vocabulary Integration**

The metadata model relies heavily on ontologies and controlled vocabularies to make values unambiguous. The `OntologyTerm` value object (Chapter 9) is the standard way to refer to a concept.

### **17.7.1 Ontology Term Selection and Annotation**

When a metadata field (e.g., `diseaseCondition`) requires an ontology term, the user interface should offer an autocomplete search across the designated ontologies. The selected term is stored as a `curie`, `uri`, and `label`. The system records the `ontologyAcronym` and the version/date of the ontology used. This provenance is crucial for long‑term resolvability.

### **17.7.2 Vocabulary Governance**

Controlled vocabularies for fields like `resourceType`, `studyDesign`, `interventionType` are maintained by the ONRR governance body as `Vocabulary` entities. They can be extended through a community proposal process. The `Vocabulary` itself is versioned, and the active version is referenced in the `MetadataProfile`.

### **17.7.3 Cross‑Ontology Mapping**

The domain supports `OntologyMapping` records that link equivalent terms across ontologies. For example, a disease in MONDO might be mapped to its DOID counterpart. The Knowledge Graph uses these mappings for query expansion, ensuring that a search for a DOID term also retrieves resources annotated with the MONDO equivalent.

---

## **17.8 Persistent Identifiers in Metadata**

The use of persistent identifiers (PIDs) is deeply embedded in the metadata model. Beyond the resource’s own DOI, the following PIDs are standard in ONRR metadata:

* Researcher: `ORCID` (stored as `orcid` in `Creator`).  
* Institution: `ROR ID` (in `affiliation` of `Creator`, and in `fundingReferences` for recipient institution).  
* Funder: `FundRef ID` (in `fundingReferences.funderIdentifier`).  
* Grant: `awardNumber` combined with `funderIdentifier` forms a unique grant reference; ideally a DOI for grants when available.  
* Dataset: `DOI` (in `relatedIdentifiers` with `relationType` `IsSupplementTo`).  
* Protocol: `DOI` or `protocols.io` URI.  
* Clinical Trial: `NCT number`, `EudraCT number`.  
* Sample/Resource: `RRID` for antibodies, cell lines, etc.

The metadata model treats all PIDs as typed identifiers, allowing validation and linking.

---

## **17.9 Mapping to External Standards**

For export and interoperability, ONRR metadata records are mapped to several widely used standards. These mappings are defined in the domain model to guarantee consistency, but are applied by the `Integration & Interoperability` context.

### **17.9.1 DataCite Mapping**

DataCite is the primary external standard because ONRR registers DOIs for publications and datasets. The mapping is straightforward:

| ONRR Field | DataCite Property |
| :---- | :---- |
| `title` | `title` |
| `creators` | `creator` (with `nameIdentifier` for ORCID) |
| `datePublished` | `publicationYear` |
| `publisher` | `publisher` |
| `resourceType` | `resourceTypeGeneral` (`Text`, `Dataset`, etc.) |
| `relatedIdentifiers` | `relatedIdentifier` |
| `rights` | `rights` (with `rightsURI`) |
| `subjects` | `subject` (with `subjectScheme` for ontology) |
| `fundingReferences` | `fundingReference` |
| `description` | `description` (with `descriptionType` `Abstract`) |
| `version` | `version` |
| `language` | `language` |

### **17.9.2 Dublin Core Mapping**

Dublin Core is used for OAI‑PMH harvesting.

| ONRR Field | Dublin Core Element |
| :---- | :---- |
| `title` | `dc:title` |
| `creators` | `dc:creator` |
| `description` | `dc:description` |
| `datePublished` | `dc:date` |
| `publisher` | `dc:publisher` |
| `resourceType` | `dc:type` |
| `subjects` | `dc:subject` |
| `rights` | `dc:rights` |
| `language` | `dc:language` |
| `relatedIdentifiers` | `dc:relation` |
| `fundingReferences` | `dc:contributor` (with role) |

### **17.9.3 [schema.org](https://schema.org/) Mapping**

[schema.org](https://schema.org/) markup is embedded in landing pages for Google Dataset Search and general SEO.

| ONRR Field | [schema.org](https://schema.org/) Property |
| :---- | :---- |
| `title` | `name` |
| `creators` | `author` (Person with `@id` as ORCID URL) |
| `description` | `description` |
| `datePublished` | `datePublished` |
| `publisher` | `publisher` |
| `subjects` | `about` (DefinedTerm with `termCode`) |
| `rights` | `license` |
| `version` | `version` |
| `fundingReferences` | `funder` |

### **17.9.4 RO‑Crate Profile**

RO‑Crate is used for packaging datasets and their metadata for transfer to external repositories. ONRR metadata is mapped to the RO‑Crate JSON‑LD context, preserving all relationships, provenance, and PIDs.

---

## **17.10 FAIR Compliance and Maturity Model**

ONRR commits to the FAIR principles. The metadata model is designed to achieve high scores on the RDA FAIR Data Maturity Model.

### **17.10.1 Findability (F)**

* F1: (Meta)data are assigned a globally unique and persistent identifier. → ONRR assigns DOIs to publications and datasets.  
* F2: Data are described with rich metadata. → The core schema plus profiles provide rich descriptors.  
* F3: Metadata clearly and explicitly include the identifier of the data they describe. → The `MetadataRecord` references the entity’s DOI.  
* F4: (Meta)data are registered or indexed in a searchable resource. → ONRR is a searchable registry; metadata is exposed via APIs and sitemaps.

### **17.10.2 Accessibility (A)**

* A1: (Meta)data are retrievable by their identifier using a standardised communications protocol. → DOIs resolve to landing pages; data is accessible via HTTP.  
* A1.1: The protocol is open, free, and universally implementable. → HTTP, REST APIs.  
* A1.2: The protocol allows for an authentication and authorization procedure, where necessary. → Restricted datasets are protected behind DAC workflows (Section 16.6.2).  
* A2: Metadata are accessible, even when the data are no longer available. → ONRR retains metadata for retracted publications and datasets.

### **17.10.3 Interoperability (I)**

* I1: (Meta)data use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation. → JSON, JSON‑LD.  
* I2: (Meta)data use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles. → ONRR uses ontologies and controlled vocabularies.  
* I3: (Meta)data include qualified references to other (meta)data. → `relatedIdentifiers`, provenance chains.

### **17.10.4 Reusability (R)**

* R1: (Meta)data are richly described with a plurality of accurate and relevant attributes. → Core‑plus‑profiles.  
* R1.1: (Meta)data are released with a clear and accessible data usage license. → `rights` field mandatory.  
* R1.2: (Meta)data are associated with detailed provenance. → `provenance` field mandatory.  
* R1.3: (Meta)data meet domain‑relevant community standards. → Profiles are community‑governed.

ONRR will publish a FAIR compliance dashboard tracking aggregate scores across its corpus.

---

## **17.11 Metadata for Machines: APIs and Harvesting**

While this document is technology‑neutral, the domain model defines the *capabilities* that the metadata must support for machine consumers.

* Search and Discovery API: A well‑defined query interface that returns metadata records in multiple formats (JSON, XML, DataCite, [schema.org](https://schema.org/)). This is a domain requirement of the `Publication & Discovery` context.  
* Harvesting Endpoint: Support for OAI‑PMH for institutional repositories and aggregators. This ensures ONRR metadata flows into large‑scale discovery systems like OpenAIRE and Google Scholar.  
* Webhooks and Notifications: Machine consumers can subscribe to domain events (e.g., `PublicationCreated`, `DatasetPublished`) to receive real‑time metadata updates (via the `Integration` context).  
* Bulk Metadata Export: Periodic snapshots of the entire public corpus in DataCite XML and RO‑Crate for archival purposes.

These capabilities are part of the domain because they directly affect FAIR compliance and stakeholder needs.

---

## **17.12 Future Evolution of the Metadata Model**

The metadata model is expected to evolve in several directions:

* Dynamic Profiles: A governance process enabling communities to propose new profiles or profile versions, with automated validation and gradual rollout.  
* Richer Provenance: Adoption of the full W3C PROV‑O ontology for provenance, allowing detailed queries about how data was derived.  
* AI‑Assisted Metadata Generation: Use of large language models to suggest subject terms, keywords, and even structured descriptions from free text, subject to curator approval.  
* Bioschemas and [Schema.org](https://schema.org/) Extensions: Deeper adoption of Bioschemas for biomedical resources, enabling richer markup for search engines.  
* Sensitive Data Metadata: Development of a metadata profile for describing the sensitivity level of data, including differential privacy guarantees, to facilitate responsible AI training.  
* Multilingual Metadata: Support for metadata in multiple languages, with the `language` field becoming per‑field rather than per‑record to accommodate international contributions.

The metadata model’s core‑plus‑profiles architecture is designed to accommodate this evolution without breaking backward compatibility.

# **CHAPTER 18: FUTURE DOMAIN EVOLUTION**

## **18.1 Introduction: The Living Domain Model**

A domain model is not a monument carved in stone; it is a living representation of the business that must adapt as scientific practice, regulatory requirements, community expectations, and technological capabilities evolve. The Open Negative Results Registry is conceived for a multi‑decade lifespan, during which the research ecosystem will undergo transformations that we can only partially anticipate. This chapter addresses the evolution of the ONRR domain model itself—how it can grow, incorporate new concepts, and respond to external change while preserving the integrity of the existing corpus, the trust of its stakeholders, and the backward compatibility of its interfaces.

We begin by establishing the core principle that governs all future change: backward compatibility must never be sacrificed, but must be achieved without freezing the domain. We then explore a range of anticipated domain changes, each grounded in current trends in open science, and we articulate the explicit extensibility mechanisms—metadata profiles, bounded context refinement, event versioning, and governance‑controlled policy updates—that enable safe, incremental evolution. Finally, we outline a strategic roadmap that aligns domain evolution with the long‑term vision of ONRR as the global negative‑results commons.

---

## **18.2 The Backward Compatibility Imperative**

ONRR’s central value proposition is the creation of a permanent, citable scientific record. A negative result published today must remain resolvable, interpretable, and comparable with a negative result published ten years hence. Any change to the domain model that breaks this continuity—whether by altering the meaning of a field, removing a previously mandatory attribute, or changing the resolution rules of a persistent identifier—would erode the very trust that the registry seeks to build.

Therefore, the domain model is governed by a strict backward compatibility policy that imposes the following constraints on all future evolution:

* No field semantics shall be altered in a breaking way. A field that was used to represent “disease condition” in 2026 must continue to represent that concept, even if a newer, richer representation is introduced. The old field may be deprecated (i.e., no longer used in new submissions) but its meaning in existing records is immutable.  
* No entity shall be removed from the model. An entity that was part of the published corpus cannot be deleted from the domain, as that would orphan audit records, citation links, and knowledge graph nodes. Entities may be superseded or merged, but their historical identity remains.  
* All domain events remain replayable. The event store is append‑only. New event types may be added; existing event payloads may be extended with optional fields; but no field may be removed or have its type changed. Event consumers must be tolerant of unknown fields.  
* Persistent identifiers must resolve in perpetuity. ONRR’s commitment to DOIs, ORCIDs, ROR IDs, and other PIDs means that if a PID scheme evolves, the registry must maintain resolvability of the old form or transparently redirect.  
* Metadata schemas are versioned. The core schema and all profiles carry explicit version numbers. Old records remain valid against the schema version under which they were published. The active schema version applies only to new submissions; existing records are never retroactively invalidated.

These guarantees are not merely technical promises; they are encoded as domain invariants that the governance body must uphold. Any proposal that would violate backward compatibility must be rejected, or accompanied by a migration plan that preserves the original state (e.g., by creating a new entity type and mapping the old one to it without altering the old entity’s persistent identifier or metadata).

---

## **18.3 Anticipated Domain Changes**

While we cannot predict the future, we can identify high‑probability developments based on current trends in open science, biomedical research, and scholarly communication. For each, we describe the likely impact on the ONRR domain and the pre‑existing extensibility mechanisms that will accommodate it.

### **18.3.1 Pre‑Registration as a Core Workflow**

Currently, the domain supports experiment pre‑registration through the standalone `Experiment` aggregate, but the primary workflow is retrospective result registration. Over the next decade, we anticipate that pre‑registration will become the dominant entry point, driven by funder mandates and cultural shifts toward Registered Reports. The domain will evolve to make the `Experiment` aggregate a first‑class citizen with its own DOI, independent discovery, and a dedicated pre‑registration review process. This will be achieved by:

* Splitting the `Experiment` lifecycle out of the `Submission` bounded context into a dedicated `Pre‑registration Context`, with its own state machine and review workflow.  
* Allowing a pre‑registered experiment to serve as the template for a future `Submission`, automatically populating the hypothesis, protocol, and methodology fields.  
* Introducing `ProtocolDeviation` entities that capture any differences between the pre‑registered plan and the executed study, strengthening the evidence chain.

Because the `Experiment` already exists as an entity and the `Submission` already references it, this evolution is additive: it enriches existing relationships without breaking them.

### **18.3.2 Enhanced Replication and Evidence Synthesis**

As the corpus of negative results grows, its primary value will shift from individual registration to collective evidence synthesis. We anticipate the domain will incorporate:

* Meta‑Analysis Entities: A `MetaAnalysis` aggregate that groups multiple related submissions (negative and positive) on the same hypothesis, automatically or through curator action, producing a synthesised effect‑size estimate. Meta‑analyses will be citable research objects in their own right.  
* Replication Chains: Explicit linking of replications into directed graphs (`ReplicationChain`), allowing a reader to see the full history of attempts to reproduce a finding. Each replication becomes a node in the chain, with a `confirms` or `refutes` relationship to its target.  
* Evidence Grading: Adoption of formal evidence grading systems (e.g., GRADE for clinical research) as a domain value object that can be attached to a `Result` based on the cumulative weight of evidence, including negative results.

These additions will be modelled as new aggregates and value objects, referencing existing entities. The knowledge graph will gain new edge types to support evidence synthesis queries.

### **18.3.3 Integration with Decentralised Identity (DIDs) and Web3**

The current model uses ORCID as the primary researcher identifier, orchestrated through the `Identity & Access` context. We expect that within the next five to ten years, self‑sovereign identity (SSI) based on Decentralised Identifiers (DIDs) and verifiable credentials will become mainstream in research. The domain will adapt by:

* Introducing a `DecentralizedIdentifier` value object as an alternative to `ORCID`.  
* Allowing a `Researcher` to link multiple identifier types, with the governance policy specifying which is primary for display.  
* Using verifiable credentials for role assertions (e.g., a university issues a credential stating that this DID belongs to a faculty member), enabling more automated institution verification.

The `ORCID` value object will remain, ensuring that legacy records are unaffected. The `Researcher` aggregate gains a collection of `IdentityAssertion` value objects that can hold any type of verified identifier.

### **18.3.4 AI and Machine Learning in the Domain**

Artificial intelligence is already beginning to assist in scientific writing, peer review, and data analysis. In the ONRR domain, we foresee AI becoming a domain concept in the following ways:

* AI‑Generated Metadata Suggestions: An `AIAnnotationSuggestion` entity (owned by the Metadata context) that proposes ontology terms, keywords, and even structured result summaries. These suggestions are reviewed by a human curator before becoming authoritative metadata.  
* Automated Quality Checks: The `DataQualityReport` may be enriched by AI‑driven checks that flag potential statistical errors, image duplications, or text‑plagiarism. The AI model and its version become part of the report’s provenance.  
* AI‑Authored Submissions: As AI systems begin to conduct experiments autonomously (e.g., in drug discovery or materials science), the domain may need to accommodate a non‑human `Researcher` subtype, with appropriate attribution and accountability rules.

These changes will be managed by extending existing entities (e.g., adding an `aiModel` field to `DataQualityReport`) and by introducing new value objects, without breaking the human‑centric core.

### **18.3.5 Regulatory and Legal Evolution**

Clinical trial transparency regulations (e.g., EU Clinical Trials Regulation, WHO Joint Statement) are progressively mandating the public posting of all results, including negative ones, within tight timeframes. ONRR will likely become a recognised results repository for these regulators, requiring:

* Regulatory Submission Mapping: A `RegulatoryCompliance` entity that maps an ONRR submission to the specific reporting requirements of a regulation (e.g., “EU CTR Annex V”), ensuring that all mandatory fields are present.  
* Auditable Timestamping: For regulatory compliance, the exact time of submission, review completion, and publication becomes legally significant. The domain already provides immutable `Timestamp` value objects; these will be augmented with trusted timestamping (e.g., blockchain anchoring) as a domain service.  
* Data Sharing Mandates: New `AccessRights` categories may be introduced (e.g., `RegulatoryRestricted`), along with automated data‑sharing agreements with regulatory agencies.

These changes will primarily affect the `Governance` and `Metadata` contexts, with new metadata fields and vocabulary terms.

### **18.3.6 Federated and Distributed Architecture**

While the current domain model assumes a single logical registry, the future may involve a federation of interoperable negative‑results registries—national, institutional, or discipline‑specific—that share a common core model. The domain will evolve to support:

* Federated Identifiers: A `RegistryPrefix` value object that scopes an identifier to a specific ONRR node, enabling global uniqueness without central coordination.  
* Cross‑Registry Replication and Linking: The `relatedIdentifiers` field will be extended to support relationships that span federated registries, with explicit trust models for cross‑registry assertions.  
* Distributed Knowledge Graph: The `KnowledgeGraphNode` aggregate may be extended to reference nodes in other registries’ graphs, using Linked Data principles.

Federation is an additive change: new value objects and relationship types are introduced, but the existing intra‑registry semantics remain unchanged.

### **18.3.7 Community‑Governed Policy as a Domain Entity**

At present, governance policies (e.g., embargo maximum, required number of reviews) are referenced by version, but the policies themselves are not fully modelled as domain entities. A future evolution will create a `GovernancePolicy` aggregate that:

* Is versioned and approved through community voting (`PolicyProposal`, `PolicyVote` entities).  
* Is referenced at submission time and locked for that submission.  
* Can be queried by API consumers to understand the rules under which a publication was made.  
* Triggers domain events (`PolicyEnacted`) that cause bounded contexts to reconfigure their validation rules and state machine guards.

This change replaces implicit, hard‑coded policy values with an explicit, auditable policy domain, enhancing transparency.

---

## **18.4 Extensibility Mechanisms**

The ONRR domain model embeds several deliberate extensibility mechanisms that enable safe, incremental evolution without requiring a “big bang” redesign. These mechanisms are not afterthoughts; they are fundamental to the domain’s long‑term viability.

### **18.4.1 Metadata Profiles and Schema Versioning**

The core‑plus‑profiles metadata architecture (Chapter 17) is the primary vehicle for accommodating new scientific domains and enriched descriptions. A new profile can be developed by a community of practice, reviewed by the governance body, and published as an active profile. Existing records are unaffected; new submissions can opt into the new profile. Profiles can deprecate fields, add new ones, and refine controlled vocabularies—all within the versioned schema system that ensures old records remain valid.

### **18.4.2 Bounded Context Refinement**

The bounded contexts defined in Chapter 6 are not static. As the domain’s complexity grows, a context may be split (e.g., extracting a `Pre‑registration Context` from `Submission`), or a new context may be introduced (e.g., an `Evidence Synthesis Context`). The domain events that connect contexts are versioned and designed for eventual consistency, so splitting or adding contexts does not disrupt existing workflows. The anti‑corruption layers between contexts protect each context’s internal model from external change.

### **18.4.3 Aggregate and Entity Extension**

New entities and value objects can be added to the domain without altering existing aggregates, provided they are introduced as standalone aggregates (like `MetaAnalysis`) or as optional children of existing aggregates (like adding a `RegulatoryCompliance` entity to the `Submission` aggregate). The key rule is: never remove or rename an existing attribute; only add new optional ones. This is enforced by the domain governance process.

### **18.4.4 Domain Event Versioning**

As described in Chapter 13, domain events employ a versioning strategy that allows new event types to be added and existing events to gain optional fields. Consumers use the tolerant reader pattern. When a breaking change is unavoidable, a new event type is created (e.g., `SubmissionSubmittedV2`), and both are published during a transition period. This ensures that downstream contexts can migrate at their own pace.

### **18.4.5 Vocabulary and Ontology Evolution**

Controlled vocabularies and ontologies evolve externally—new terms are added, old terms are deprecated, and meaning drifts over time. The ONRR domain shields itself from this churn by:

* Caching the state of an ontology term at the time of annotation (storing the `termLabel` and `ontologyVersion` along with the CURIE).  
* Supporting `OntologyMapping` records that preserve semantic equivalence across ontology versions.  
* Using the `Vocabulary` entity to version the allowed values for metadata fields, with clear deprecation policies.

### **18.4.6 Governance‑Controlled Policy Parameters**

Many business rules (Chapter 12) contain configurable parameters: maximum embargo length, minimum number of reviews, reviewer invitation expiry. These parameters are not hard‑coded in the domain; they are references to the active `GovernancePolicy`. Changing a policy parameter updates the policy version and immediately affects new submissions, while existing submissions continue to be governed by the policy version in effect at their submission time. This mechanism is itself an extensibility feature: new policy parameters can be added without altering the structure of the business rules.

---

## **18.5 Strategic Roadmap for Domain Evolution**

The following roadmap outlines the anticipated major milestones in the evolution of the ONRR domain model over the next decade. It is not a project plan; it is a strategic forecast that guides the community and the governance body in setting priorities.

### **Phase 1: Consolidation and Adoption (2026–2028)**

* Goal: Stabilise the core domain model and achieve critical mass of submissions.  
* Focus: Refine the biomedical metadata profile based on early adopter feedback. Enhance the `Dataset` aggregate with robust versioning and provenance. Begin integration with major trial registries ([ClinicalTrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/), EU CTR).  
* Domain Changes: Minor additions to metadata fields; no structural changes. First community‑proposed vocabulary extensions.

### **Phase 2: Pre‑Registration and Replication (2028–2030)**

* Goal: Make pre‑registration and replication the primary workflows.  
* Focus: Extract the `Pre‑registration Context` from `Submission`. Introduce the `ReplicationChain` and `MetaAnalysis` aggregates. Develop the social sciences and physical sciences metadata profiles.  
* Domain Changes: Additive: new bounded contexts, new aggregates, new profiles. The original `Submission` context remains intact for retrospective registration.

### **Phase 3: Evidence Synthesis and AI Integration (2030–2033)**

* Goal: Transform ONRR from a registry into a knowledge‑synthesis platform.  
* Focus: Implement evidence grading and AI‑assisted metadata. Introduce the `MetaAnalysis` publishing workflow. Begin integration with decentralised identity providers.  
* Domain Changes: New value objects (`EvidenceGrade`, `AIAnnotationSuggestion`), new entity subtypes (`AIResearcher`), enhanced knowledge graph reasoning capabilities.

### **Phase 4: Global Federation (2033–2036)**

* Goal: Enable a global, federated network of interoperable negative‑results registries.  
* Focus: Define federation protocols and the `RegistryPrefix` identity scheme. Develop cross‑registry trust models. Publish the ONRR core domain model as an open standard (e.g., an OMG or RDA recommendation).  
* Domain Changes: Core model stabilised; federation layer added as a new bounded context. Existing entities gain federated identity attributes.

Throughout all phases, backward compatibility remains inviolable. Each phase’s changes are additive, building on the solid foundation established by this initial domain model.

---

## **18.6 The Domain Model as a Community Standard**

The ultimate expression of domain evolution is the transformation of the ONRR domain model from an internal specification into a community standard—a reference model that other registries, institutional repositories, and publisher systems can adopt. To achieve this, the following steps are anticipated:

* Publication of this Domain Model Specification as a formal document with a persistent DOI, independent of the software repository.  
* Canonical representation of the domain model in a machine‑readable ontology (e.g., OWL, SHACL) that enables automated validation and mapping.  
* Community governance of the domain model through a Domain Council with representation from all major stakeholder groups.  
* Open change proposal process (Domain Change Requests, DCRs) modelled on the W3C or IETF processes, ensuring transparency and consensus.  
* Conformance certification for third‑party systems that claim to implement the ONRR model, ensuring interoperability.

These steps will cement the ONRR domain model as a lasting contribution to the open science infrastructure, independent of any single implementation.

---

## **18.7 Concluding Remarks**

The Open Negative Results Registry Domain Model Specification presented in this document is the product of rigorous analysis, deep collaboration with domain experts, and the application of proven software architecture principles. It defines, in precise and exhaustive detail, the business concepts, rules, and processes that constitute the registry. It provides a single source of truth for all stakeholders, enabling the construction of a system that faithfully serves the scientific community’s need for transparency, reproducibility, and completeness in the scientific record.

Yet this specification is not an end; it is a beginning. The domain will grow as science grows. The extensibility mechanisms, governance structures, and backward compatibility guarantees described in this final chapter are designed to ensure that ONRR can thrive for decades, adapting to new discoveries while preserving the integrity of every negative result ever registered.

We invite the global research community to engage with this model, to propose improvements, to develop new profiles for their disciplines, and to help ONRR achieve its vision of a world where negative results are valued, shared, and built upon—for the benefit of science and society.

---

*End of Chapter 18 – approximately 14 pages in printed form.*

---

# **MANIFESTVM ET DISCLAMATIO**

## **A Manifesto and Disclaimer for the Open Negative Results Registry**

Author: Ciprian Stefan Plesca  
Status: Independent Researcher  
Date: 2026-07-23  
Repository: [https://github.com/Ciprian-LocalPulse/open-negative-results-registry](https://github.com/Ciprian-LocalPulse/open-negative-results-registry)

---

### **PREAMBLE**

*Ubi scientia, ibi lux.*  
Where there is knowledge, there is light. The pursuit of truth through systematic inquiry is among the highest callings of the human intellect. Yet the architecture of modern scholarly communication, shaped by historical accident and commercial imperatives, has long suppressed a vital component of scientific truth: the negative result. This repository, and the Open Negative Results Registry (ONRR) it documents, is offered as a corrective—a public, open, and incorruptible archive dedicated to the proposition that a null finding, a failed replication, or an inconclusive endpoint is not a failure of science but a necessary and honourable contribution to the collective edifice of human understanding.

This document serves simultaneously as a Manifesto—a public declaration of the principles, motivations, and aspirations that animate this project—and as a Disclaimer—a formal, legally conscious statement that delineates the scope of responsibility, the terms of intellectual engagement, and the limitations inherent in a work offered *pro bono publico* (for the public good).

I, Ciprian Stefan Plesca, acting as an independent researcher, unaffiliated with any institution, university, or commercial entity, and motivated solely by the conviction that open science is a moral imperative for the twenty‑first century, set forth this declaration before the community of scholars, developers, funders, and citizens who will interact with this repository.

---

### **I. DECLARATIO AVCTORIS (Author’s Declaration)**

I am an independent researcher. I hold no formal academic appointment, nor am I bound by the policies of any funding body, corporate sponsor, or government agency. This independence is not a weakness; it is the wellspring of the project’s integrity. Unencumbered by institutional pressures to publish selectively, to inflate effect sizes, or to pursue fashionable lines of inquiry, I am free to dedicate my labour to the thankless but essential task of building infrastructure for the “file‑drawer” of science.

Every line of code, every architectural decision, and every word of documentation contained in this repository is the product of my personal commitment to the principles articulated below. I claim no financial interest, nor do I seek personal enrichment, from the deployment or adoption of this system. My sole reward is the knowledge that, should this registry prevent even a single redundant clinical trial, spare a single experimental animal, or save a single researcher‑year of wasted effort, the work will have been justified.

---

### **II. PROFESSIO PROPOSITI (Statement of Purpose)**

The purpose of the Open Negative Results Registry is to provide a persistent, citable, machine‑actionable, and community‑governed public infrastructure for the registration, peer review, and dissemination of scientific results that do not support the initial hypothesis—negative, null, and inconclusive outcomes—across all disciplines of empirical inquiry.

The specific objectives of this repository are:

1. To publish and maintain a complete, technology‑agnostic Domain Model Specification that defines the business‑domain of a negative‑results registry with the rigour of formal ontology, employing the principles of Domain‑Driven Design, FAIR data stewardship, and international standards for scholarly metadata.  
2. To serve as a reference implementation blueprint that can be realised in any programming language, deployed on any cloud or on‑premise infrastructure, and adapted to the governance requirements of universities, hospitals, funding agencies, and supranational research organisations.  
3. To advocate, by example, for the revaluation of negative results as first‑class scientific objects, thereby correcting the epistemological distortion introduced by publication bias and restoring the completeness of the scientific record.  
4. To offer this work to humanity without restriction, save for the requirement that any derivative works likewise remain open and that attribution to the original author be preserved.

---

### **III. PRINCIPIA FVNDAMENTALIA (Fundamental Principles)**

The following principles are not negotiable. They constitute the philosophical bedrock upon which every technical choice in this repository has been made.

1. Scientia aperta omnibus (Science open to all).  
   Knowledge produced with public funds, or intended for the public benefit, must be freely accessible to all persons, without paywall, embargo, or discrimination. The ONRR is designed from its foundations to be an open‑access, open‑data, open‑source system.  
2. Nullum eventum negativum oblivioni tradendum (No negative result shall be consigned to oblivion).  
   A well‑conducted experiment that yields a null outcome is as valuable to the scientific community as one that confirms a fashionable hypothesis. The ONRR bestows upon negative results the same dignity—persistent identifiers, peer review, and citability—that positive results enjoy.  
3. Separatio consiliorum ab instrumentis (Separation of design from implementation).  
   The domain model documented in this repository is intentionally independent of any programming language, database engine, or framework. This separation ensures that the specification remains useful for decades, surviving the churn of technology cycles.  
4. Integritas perpetua monumentorum (Perpetual integrity of the record).  
   Once a result is published in the ONRR, it becomes part of the permanent scholarly record. It may be retracted, corrected, or superseded, but it may never be silently altered or expunged. The audit trail is immutable.  
5. Gubernatio communitaria (Community governance).  
   The long‑term stewardship of the ONRR must rest in the hands of a diverse, international, and transparent community of researchers, curators, librarians, and ethicists—not in the hands of any single individual, institution, or corporation. This repository is the seed; the community will be the tree.

---

### **IV. DE PROPRIETATE INTELLECTVALI ET LICENTIA (Intellectual Property and Licensing)**

All original content in this repository—including but not limited to the Domain Model Specification, architectural documentation, JSON schemas, governance framework, and the present Manifesto—is authored by Ciprian Stefan Plesca and is released under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0) , unless otherwise noted.

Under this license, any person or organisation is free to:

* Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium, mode, or format for any purpose, even commercially.  
* Adapt — remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, even commercially.

Provided they comply with the following condition:

* Attribution — They must give appropriate credit to Ciprian Stefan Plesca as the original author, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made. They may do so in any reasonable manner, but not in any way that suggests the licensor endorses them or their use.

The choice of the CC BY license is deliberate. It embodies the principle of *scientia aperta* while preserving the moral right of the author to be recognised as the originator of this work. There is no requirement for share‑alike (copyleft), for the author believes that even proprietary systems should be able to implement open standards for negative‑result registration—as long as they acknowledge the source.

Any software source code that may be included in this repository in the future shall be licensed under the MIT License, a permissive open‑source license compatible with both academic and commercial use.

---

### **V. DISCLAMATIO CVSTODIAE ET OBLIGATIONIS (Disclaimer of Warranty and Liability)**

This repository and all of its contents are provided “as is”, without warranty of any kind, express or implied, including but not limited to the warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, title, and non‑infringement. In no event shall the author, Ciprian Stefan Plesca, be held liable for any claim, damages, or other liability, whether in an action of contract, tort, or otherwise, arising from, out of, or in connection with the use of the materials in this repository or the deployment of any system derived from them.

Specifically, but without limitation:

1. No guarantee of fitness for regulatory compliance. The specifications in this repository are intended to describe a best‑practice scientific information system. They do not constitute legal advice, nor do they guarantee compliance with any specific regulation, including but not limited to GDPR, HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, EU Clinical Trials Regulation, or any other national or supranational law. Any entity implementing the ONRR must perform its own legal and regulatory analysis.  
2. No guarantee of security or infallibility. The domain model and associated documentation describe a system for managing scientific information. The author makes no representation that an implementation will be free from security vulnerabilities, data loss, or system errors. The implementer bears sole responsibility for the security and operational integrity of any deployed instance.  
3. No endorsement. The inclusion of references to external standards, registries, ontologies, or organisations (e.g., ORCID, DataCite, ROR, OBO Foundry, FAIR Data Maturity Model) does not imply endorsement by those organisations, nor do they endorse this work unless explicitly stated.  
4. No clinical or scientific advice. Nothing in this repository constitutes medical, scientific, or professional advice. The ONRR is a repository for scientific results; it does not validate or endorse the truth of any registered result beyond the peer‑review and curation processes defined in its domain model.  
5. Limitation of liability. To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, the author disclaims any liability for direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, or special damages arising out of or in any way connected with the use of this repository or any derivative work.

---

### **VI. DE OFFICIIS ETHICIS (Ethical Commitments)**

The author commits to the following ethical standards in the maintenance and evolution of this repository:

* Transparency of authorship: Every substantive contribution to the domain model or documentation shall be clearly attributed. Ghost‑writing, undisclosed corporate sponsorship, or covert influence is antithetical to the project’s ethos.  
* Inclusivity of voice: While I, as the originating author, bear ultimate responsibility for the coherence of the specification, I welcome and will actively seek critique, correction, and extension from researchers of diverse disciplines, nationalities, genders, and career stages. The ONRR cannot serve humanity if it is built by a single mind in isolation.  
* No “dark patterns”: The ONRR user experience, as described in the domain model (state machines, validation rules, permissions), shall never be designed to coerce researchers into registering only positive results, to obscure negative findings, or to manipulate metrics for commercial gain.  
* Environmental responsibility: The architecture documented here favours eventual consistency, caching, and efficient event‑driven communication patterns. Any reference implementation should minimise its carbon footprint, respecting the principle that digital infrastructure has material ecological consequences.

---

### **VII. APPELLATIO AD COMMVNITATEM SCIENTIARVM (Call to the Scientific Community)**

This repository is an offering. It is not a product, nor a service, nor a startup. It is a seed cast into the soil of the commons, in the hope that it will germinate into a living institution—owned by no one and stewarded by all.

I call upon:

* Researchers, especially early‑career scientists who suffer most acutely from the file‑drawer problem, to demand that their institutions provide them with the means to register negative results, and to adopt or adapt the ONRR model.  
* Software architects and developers, to engage with the Domain Model Specification, to critique its abstractions, to build reference implementations, and to contribute to its evolution through pull requests and open debate.  
* Librarians and data stewards, to help craft the metadata profiles, ontology mappings, and curation workflows that will make the ONRR a FAIR‑native resource.  
* Funding agencies and philanthropic organisations, to mandate the registration of all results, positive and negative, from the research they support, and to provide the modest resources needed to sustain a community‑governed registry.  
* Publishers and editors, to adopt the ONRR as a companion registry for null findings, to link journal articles to their negative‑result counterparts, and to reconsider the obsession with novelty that has so warped the incentive structures of science.

If this repository inspires even a single researcher to share a negative result that would otherwise have mouldered in a file drawer; if it prevents a single clinical trial from being repeated needlessly; if it contributes, in however small a measure, to bending the arc of science towards greater honesty, transparency, and completeness—then the labour of its creation will have been repaid a thousandfold.

---

### **VIII. CLAVSVLA (Closing)**

*Non nobis solum nati sumus.*  
We are not born for ourselves alone.

Science is the greatest cooperative enterprise in the history of our species. It transcends borders, generations, and individual lifespans. But science works only when the evidence is complete. A literature that omits negative results is a map with half the terrain erased—and a map that cannot be trusted leads its users into swamps of wasted effort, duplication, and false certainty.

This repository is my contribution to the redrawing of that map. It is offered without condition, without reservation, and without expectation of reward. It is placed in the public trust, to be used, improved, and ultimately superseded by a community that will carry its vision further than any single individual ever could.

May it serve the truth. May it serve humanity.

Ciprian Stefan Plesca  
Independent Researcher  
July 23, 2026  
contact@agentflow-enterprise.com
